nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to mid-depth tropical reef slopes and lagoons with moderate to strong water movement; occurs on hard substrate in areas with access to moderate light

decorPreferences: open spacing from neighboring corals (15+ cm clearance)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis. Target feeding 1–2 times per week with small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafood, zooplankton-based coral foods, or small LPS pellets — is generally advised to support growth and coloration. Polyps typically extend to capture food during or after lights-out.

goodWith: reef fish (clownfish, gobies, blennies, peaceful wrasses)
non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of sweeper tentacle range
avoidWith: Torch Corals (Euphyllia glabrescens) — documented to damage hammer corals aggressively
any coral or invertebrate within sweeper tentacle range at night
large angelfish that may nip at polyps
certain butterflyfish that nip at LPS polyps

summary: The Branching Hammer Coral is a popular LPS reef coral with distinctive hammer- or anchor-shaped tentacle tips. More forgiving than its Wall Hammer relative, it tends to suit intermediate reef-keepers seeking a visually striking centerpiece coral.
careNotes:
TAXONOMY NOTE: Euphyllia parancora (Veron, 1990) was reclassified to Fimbriaphyllia paraancora in a 2017 molecular phylogenetic study (published 2018 in WoRMS) that resurrected Fimbriaphyllia as a full genus separate from Euphyllia. The new family is Euphylliidae. The old name Euphyllia parancora (and variant spelling paraancora) remains dominant in the aquarium trade and is preserved as the primary scientificName here; the reclassified name is listed in alsoKnownAs.

HOBBY DISTINCTION — HAMMER vs. FROGSPAWN vs. TORCH:
• Hammer Coral (branching: E. parancora / wall: E. ancora): tentacle tips are distinctly hammer-, anchor-, or T-shaped. The branching form grows on separate branch stalks; the wall form (E. ancora) shares a continuous fused base. Branching hammer is generally considered more resilient and easier to propagate by fragging.
• Frogspawn Coral (branching: Fimbriaphyllia paradivisa / wall: F. divisa): tentacle tips are rounded and bubble-like, resembling frog eggs. Very similar husbandry to hammer.
• Torch Coral (Euphyllia glabrescens): tentacle tips are elongated, thin, and finger-like. Considered more aggressive than hammer or frogspawn — torch corals placed in proximity have been reported to damage or kill hammer corals. Hammer and frogspawn coexist more readily.

In the hobby, hammer, frogspawn, and torch are often grouped together as 'Euphyllia corals' and share broadly similar care, but Torch corals should not be mixed with hammers without very generous spacing (30+ cm).

SWEEPER TENTACLES & ALLELOPATHY: Branching Hammer Corals extend potent sweeper tentacles — typically 10–15 cm, and reportedly up to 20–25 cm when the colony is nutrient-stressed or starving. These tentacles sting and can kill adjacent corals, including SPS frags, overnight. Spacing of at least 15 cm from any other coral is widely advised; 20+ cm is more conservative. This species is also allelopathic (releases chemical irritants into the water), so frequent water changes and activated carbon are recommended in mixed reef systems.

LIGHTING: PAR 75–150 is the range most consistently cited by multiple care sources. Branching hammer corals are generally considered more tolerant of lighting fluctuations than wall hammer corals. Acclimate new specimens to their target PAR level gradually over 2–4 weeks to prevent bleaching. Placing in mid-rockwork and adjusting up or down based on polyp extension response is the commonly advised approach.

FLOW: Moderate, indirect, non-laminar flow is preferred — sufficient to create a gentle swaying motion of the tentacles without causing them to retract or whip forcefully. Direct powerhead flow pointed at the coral should be avoided.

BROWN JELLY DISEASE: E. parancora is susceptible to Brown Jelly Disease (BJD), a bacterial infection that rapidly dissolves tissue. Physical damage (shipping stress, sting contact with other corals, forceful direct flow) is the most common trigger. Affected tissue should be removed promptly with a brine or freshwater dip to prevent spread to other heads.

FRAGGING: Branching hammer corals are among the most frag-friendly LPS corals. Individual branches can be cut with bone cutters at the base; frags mount readily on plugs or rubble. If one head is lost to disease or injury, the rest of the colony is generally not affected — a key advantage over the wall form.

CALCIUM, ALKALINITY, MAGNESIUM: Consistent maintenance of calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–11 dKH), and magnesium (1250–1400 ppm) is important for skeletal growth. Sudden swings in alkalinity (more than 1 dKH per day) can cause polyp recession. A two-part dosing regimen or calcium reactor is advisable for established colonies.

CROSS-SOURCE DISAGREEMENT NOTES: Temperature upper bound varied from 25.6°C (LiveAquaria, AquAnswers) to 28.3°C (AquariumBreeder); the averaged range of 23–27°C was adopted. PAR lower bound varied from 50 to 100; 75 adopted as average. Care level ranged from easy (AquariumBreeder) to moderate (LiveAquaria); intermediate adopted as the midpoint. LiveAquaria lists placement as 'bottom'; other sources say 'middle to lower'; 'middle' adopted as the broader consensus.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting branches) is the primary propagation method in captivity and is straightforward with bone cutters or a bandsaw. Frags as small as a single head (~2 cm) will encrust and grow on frag plugs under appropriate conditions. Related species Fimbriaphyllia ancora has been documented as a gonochoric broadcast spawner with an annual sex-change strategy in the wild; similar reproductive biology is likely for F. paraancora, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the reef hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Hammer Coral, Branching
primary.notes: Primary species care sheet: care level (moderate), temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.023–1.025, lighting moderate, flow medium, placement bottom, sweeper tentacles up to 6 inches, weekly feeding. Scientific name listed as Euphyllia parancora; family listed as Caryophylliidae (outdated — corrected to Euphylliidae per WoRMS 2018).

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Hammer Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.024–1.026, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, PAR 80–150. Flow: moderate indirect. Placement: middle to lower. Feeding: 1–2× weekly. Used to resolve temperature and salinity upper-bound disagreements.

additional.name: AquariumBreeder – How to Care for Hammer Corals
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–83°F (upper end outlier), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, kH 8–12, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, PAR 75–100. Minimum tank size: 20 gal (80 L) minimum; 50+ gal (189+ L) recommended. Native range: Indo-Pacific, Philippines, Australia, Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, depths 6–40 m. Adult colony size 20–30 cm in aquaria. Sweeper tentacles up to 20 cm (8 inches). Care level: easy.

additional.name: ReefStable – Euphyllia Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 78–80°F, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm, PAR 100–150. Sweeper tentacles up to 6 inches typical; larger colonies up to 12 inches. Flow: low to medium. Care level: beginner-friendly.

additional.name: WoRMS – Fimbriaphyllia paraancora
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy: accepted name Fimbriaphyllia paraancora (Veron, 1990); synonym Euphyllia paraancora Veron, 1990; family Euphylliidae; order Scleractinia. Original description from Japan and Indo-West Pacific. Used to correct LiveAquaria's outdated family name (Caryophylliidae) and document the 2017/2018 reclassification.
